english: thet of Buddha; ye-mk'yén (ye mkhyen), mṅon-mk'yén (mngon mkhyen) = ye-śés (ye shes), mṅon-śés (mngon shes). — t'ugs-mk'yén (thugs mkhyen) is known to me only as a sbst. abstr.: the knowing, knowledge, prophetic sight, rje-btsún-gyis t'ugs-mk'yén-gyis gzigs-pa lágs-sam (rje btsun gyis thugs mkhyen gyis gzigs pa lags sam) has your reverence seen by your prophetic sight? Mil.; acc. to Cs., however, sku-mk'yén (sku mkhyen), gsuṅ-mk'yén (gsung mkhyen) and t'ugs-mk'yén (thugs mkhyen) are identical in meaning with mk'yen-mk'yén (mkhyen mkhyen), a form of entreaty which, as a Lama told me, properly has the sense: you know yourself best what is good for me! In accordance to this explanation we find in Mil. after an entreaty: blá-ma kyed mk'yen-mk'yén (bla ma khyed mkhyen mkhyen). It is gen. added without any connecting word, like our pray, or please, but sometimes it is construed with the inf.: mdzád-pa(r) mk'yen-mk'yén (mdzad pa(r) mkhyen mkhyen), please to do.

also k'raṅ (khrang) hard, solid, compact; srá-mk'raṅ-can (sra mkhrang can) gyur-méd (gyur med) Tlgy. firm, hearty, sound, of a strong and robust constitution. — mk'raṅ-pa (mkhrang pa) denotes the fourth stage of the development of the foetus Thgy.

B. and C. (Ssk. पित्त) bile, gall. — 1. the vesicle of the gall, the gall-bladder, as part of the intestines. — 2. generally: the substance of the bile, the bilious fluid, which acc. to Indo-Tibetan philosophy is connected with the element of fire, and which, conformably to its functions, is divided into five species, of which physiology gives the oddest details. — mk'ris-nád (mkhris nad) bilious disease; mk'ris-ts'ád (mkhris tshad) prob. bilious fever; gráṅ-mk'ris (grang mkhris) a feverish shivering, a chill.
W. *ṭág-mo* (*tag mo*) (Ssk. सार) hard, firm, e.g. snow; *go-ṭág-can* (*go tag can*) W. obstinate, stiffnecked, stubborn.

(not: to put a fault or crime on another Cs., but:) to hurt or offend, to annoy, to vex, ts'úr-la °k'aṅ (tshur la 'khang) we cause vexation to ourselves (by minding too much the affairs of others): °k'aṅ ('khang) animosity, °k'aṅ maṅ ('khang mang) there occur many collisions, quarrels Mil., dpon-slób re °k'aṅ byuṅ (dpon slob re 'khang byung) there arise mutual differences, animosities, between masters and scholars Mil.

esp. W. 1. to sit, to sit firm, rtai k'á-ru (rtai kha ru) on the back of a horse. — 2. to remain sitting, to stick fast, to be stopped, kept back, e.g. of a bird, rnyíus (rnyius), in a snare: rkáṅ-pa °k'ád-de gyél-ba (rkang pa 'khad de gyel ba) to get entangled with the foot so as to fall; sgo °k'ad dug (sgo 'khad dug) the door sticks. Cf. also k'ad-pa (khad pa) and bkad-pa (bkad pa).
1. to faint away, to swoon. — 2. Sch. also: to take into one's mouth.

I. sbst. 1. staff v. mk'ár-ba (mkhar ba). — 2. bronze, bell-metal, °k'ár-(bai) c'ú ('khar (bai) chu) molten, liquid bronze, °k'ár-bai mé-loṅ ('khar bai me long) a metallic mirror; °k'ar-rṅá ('khar rnga) gong, used in India and China instead of bells: Cs.: a drum of bronze; yet it is rather a large bronze disk, producing, when struck, a very loud sound like that of a bell. — °k'ar-gzhóṅ ('khar gzhong) a dish or basin of metal; °k'ar-záṅs ('khar zangs) a metallic kettle. — II. vb., in C. the same as °k'ád-pa ('khad pa). — 2. in W. intrs. to dgar-ba (dgar ba).
pf. and fut. (imp. k'ol? (khol?)), W. *kál-ce* (*kal ce*) 1. B., C., W.: to spin, bal (bal), wool, skúd-pa (skud pa) a thread, srád-bu (srad bu) yarn. — 2. W. to send, to forward, things.

to offend, insult, Bhar. (Lex. = द्रोह injury); °k'u-°k'rïg ('khu 'khrig), acc. to the context, denotes certain passions that disturb the tranquillity of the mind, such as malignity and covetousness: °k'áṅ-ba ('khang ba) is synon. — Cs.'s 'to emulate, contemn, hate, long for', and Sch.'s 'pride' I am not able to verify.

to groan, to fetch a deep sigh, not so much as a sign of pain or sorrow, but rather as a mere physical deep and hollow sound Med.; °k'ún-sgras k'áṅ-pa k'eṅs ('khun sgras khang pa khengs) he filled the house with
